candidate_problems.text: What exactly is the minimal feedback structure required for a token-predicting system to count as participating in genuine knowledge creation, rather than merely aiding human knowers? The conjecture says criticism, correction, and retention are necessary, but leaves open which kinds of feedback (human dialogue, experimental grounding, automated evaluation, model updating, institutional review) are sufficient, and at what threshold the system itself becomes an epistemic agent rather than a tool inside a larger epistemic system.

How can we distinguish, in practice and in principle, between explanations that are merely persuasive recombinations and explanations that have entered a real error-correcting selective environment? The conjecture relies on this distinction but does not provide operational criteria or metrics for detecting when apparent progress is actually epistemic overload, selection failure, or genuine knowledge growth.

Does increasing explanation-generation speed necessarily weaken epistemic selection pressure, or can new institutions and technical designs restore or even improve feedback loops at higher throughput? This is a major unresolved systems question because the conjecture identifies overload as the danger, but it remains open whether scalable criticism mechanisms can co-evolve with generation capacity, changing the balance from epistemic flooding to accelerated knowledge creation.
